items.q: Can Pythagoras' theorem be used on any triangle?
items.correct: No — it only applies to right-angled triangles.
items.misconception: Yes — it works on any triangle as long as you square the sides.
items.random_wrong: Only on equilateral triangles because all sides are equal.
items.why: Students learn the formula a^2 + b^2 = c^2 abstractly and forget the essential prerequisite — a 90 degree angle.

items.q: In Pythagoras' theorem, which side does c^2 represent?
items.correct: The hypotenuse — the longest side, opposite the right angle.
items.misconception: c^2 represents any side of the triangle as long as the other two are squared.
items.random_wrong: c^2 represents the side opposite the largest angle that is not 90 degrees.
items.why: Students treat the formula as symmetric in a, b, c, missing that c specifically denotes the hypotenuse.

items.q: What is the sum of the interior angles of any pentagon?
items.misconception: 360 degrees, because all polygons' angles sum to one full turn.
items.random_wrong: 180 degrees, the same as a triangle.
items.why: Students remember a single 'angles add up to' figure and apply it universally, or confuse interior with exterior angles (which do sum to 360).

items.q: Angles in a triangle sum to:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 180°. “90°” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: π × r². “2 × π × r” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Area of a rectangle 8 m by 5 m (m²)?
items.why: The real figure is 40. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
